title: Environmental Stewardship
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Caring for Creation
summary: Scripture presents humanity as stewards of a creation that belongs to God, called to tend and protect the earth rather than exploit it for short-term gain.
body:
The first job description in Scripture is environmental. God places Adam in the garden to work it and take care of it [verse:gen.2.15]. The Hebrew words carry the sense of serving and guarding — the human relationship to creation is one of responsibility, not ownership. The earth is the Lord's, the psalmist declares, and everything in it [verse:ps.24.1].

The dominion mandate in Genesis 1 has sometimes been misread as a license for exploitation. But biblical dominion is modeled on God's own rule — protective, generative, and concerned with flourishing [verse:gen.1.28]. A king who destroys what he governs is no king at all.

The Law of Moses included ecological provisions. Fields were to rest every seventh year [verse:lev.25.4]. Mother birds could not be taken with their young [verse:deut.22.6-7]. Even in war, fruit trees were protected from destruction. These laws reveal a God who cares about the non-human creation and expects his people to do the same.

Paul writes that creation itself groans under the weight of human sin, waiting to be liberated from its bondage to decay [verse:rom.8.19-21]. The redemption God plans is not an escape from the physical world but its renewal. Revelation envisions not the destruction of the earth but a new heaven and a new earth where God dwells with his people.

Stewardship of creation is not a political issue — it is a theological one. The earth is entrusted to us, and the One who entrusted it will ask for an accounting.

verses.annotation: Humanity's first assignment: to work and keep the garden — stewardship from the beginning.
The earth belongs to the Lord — humans are tenants, not owners.
The dominion mandate — authority modeled on God's own protective governance.
Creation groans under decay and awaits liberation — redemption includes the physical world.
The sabbatical year for the land — even the soil needs rest in God's design.
A law protecting mother birds — God's concern extends to individual creatures.
The land itself is polluted by wrongdoing — ecological and moral health are linked.

scholars.quote: Moo reads Romans 8:19-21 as Paul's assertion that the scope of redemption is cosmic, not merely personal — creation itself is included in God's plan to set things right.

Hess argues that the Genesis mandate to 'rule' over creation uses language associated with wise and just governance, not exploitation — Adam's dominion is patterned on God's own care for what he has made.

Goldingay observes that the Psalms' celebration of creation consistently locates humanity within the natural order rather than above it — we share the world with creatures that have their own relationship with the Creator.
